name: Royal Peacock Opal Mines
short_description: A premium fee dig site in the high desert where collectors can search for precious and fire opals in prepared digging areas. Known for producing quality opals with vibrant play-of-color.
description: Royal Peacock Opal Mines is one of Nevada's most accessible opal collecting destinations, located in the remote high desert of northwestern Nevada near the Black Rock Desert. The site operates as a commercial fee dig operation where visitors can work in designated pit areas to extract opal-bearing host rock and cut their own specimens. The mines are situated in an area rich with opaline deposits formed by ancient hot spring activity and silica-rich groundwater flowing through fractures in the host rock. Collectors of all experience levels can participate, with on-site staff available to help identify promising material and provide guidance. The operation has developed multiple digging areas with varying difficulty levels, making it accessible to families and serious collectors alike. Visitors should expect high desert conditions with intense sun and significant temperature swings between day and night.
history: This region of Humboldt County sits within the Basin and Range geologic province, where Tertiary volcanic activity and subsequent hydrothermal circulation created extensive silica deposits. The opal deposits here formed from silica-rich solutions that percolated through fractures in rhyolite and other volcanic host rocks, precipitating as precious opal in nodules and seams over millions of years. Indigenous peoples and early prospectors recognized the area's mineral wealth, and modern commercial operations have developed these deposits into accessible collecting sites.
tips: Focus your digging in the prepared pit areas where the opal-bearing matrix has been exposed; concentrate on areas showing color play or waxy luster in the host rock. Use a small pry bar or crack hammer to carefully work the friable host material - opals here are often found as nodules or replacements within the rock rather than in discrete seams. Look for the characteristic play-of-color that indicates precious opal, ranging from red and orange (fire opal) to the full spectrum in precious opal varieties. Bring plenty of water and sun protection; the high desert sun is intense, and the nearest shade may be limited.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 3900-4000 feet in the Black Rock Desert high plains region. Terrain is open, relatively flat to gently sloping with sparse low sagebrush and desert vegetation. Digging areas are prepared mine pits with loose to moderately hard material. Footing is relatively stable in developed dig areas but can be uneven. Walking distance from parking is typically short to modest, usually under half a mile to active digging zones.
best_season: October - April. The high desert experiences extreme heat in summer (100-110F) with intense sun and minimal shade. Winter brings cold nighttime temperatures but comfortable daytime conditions for outdoor work. Spring and fall offer the most pleasant conditions for extended digging sessions.
written_directions: From Gerlach, take Nevada Highway 447 north approximately 15-18 miles into the Black Rock Desert. Look for the Royal Peacock Opal Mines entrance sign on the right side of the road. Follow the access road to the main facility and parking area. The site is well-marked and accessible by standard passenger vehicle during dry conditions, though the final approach may be unpaved and rough.
road_conditions: Nevada Highway 447 is paved. Final access road to the mine facility is unpaved and can be rough or washboarded; may be impassable during or immediately after rain.
parking_notes: On-site parking area available at the main facility. Capacity accommodates multiple vehicles; exact spaces unknown but sufficient for typical daily traffic.
collection_rules: Fee dig operation - personal use only. Visitors pay an entry or digging fee to access prepared pit areas and keep material found. Contact the site directly for current fee structure and any per-person or weight limits. Standard BLM personal use rules apply: maximum 25 lbs per day aggregate personal collection from surrounding public lands if collecting outside fee dig areas.
quantity_limits: Varies by fee arrangement; contact site directly for current limits
rules:
Personal use only on fee dig areas
Fee required for access and digging rights
Keep what you find within site policies
No commercial collection or resale
No motorized excavation without permission
Respect all posted signage and staff instructions
nearest_services: Gerlach, approximately 15-18 miles south - small community with basic services including a general store. McDermitt, approximately 45 miles north, has more comprehensive services.
accessibility_notes: Prepared digging areas are relatively easy to access and work in. Terrain is mostly flat within excavated pits. No wheelchair accessibility to active digging zones. Visitors of all mobility levels can participate in shallow pit areas, though bent postures and reaching movements are required for effective collecting. Sturdy shoes recommended for walking between pit areas.

faq.question: What types of opal can I find at Royal Peacock Opal Mines in Nevada?
faq.answer: Royal Peacock Opal Mines is known for precious opal and fire opal. Precious opal displays the characteristic play-of-color with rainbow hues, while fire opal shows warm orange and red tones. Both types are found in the prepared digging areas as nodules or replacements within the host rock matrix.

faq.question: How much does it cost to dig for opal at Royal Peacock near Gerlach?
faq.answer: Royal Peacock operates as a fee dig site where visitors pay for access to excavated pit areas. Exact current fee amounts vary by digging area and group size. Contact the site directly for up-to-date pricing and to confirm what your fee includes in terms of material you can keep.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Royal Peacock Opal Mines?
faq.answer: No permit is required to visit and dig at Royal Peacock - it is a commercial fee dig operation where payment grants you digging rights in designated areas. The site is private-operated, so standard BLM permits do not apply here.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to dig for opal in northwestern Nevada?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for digging at Royal Peacock. Daytime temperatures are comfortable, and you avoid the extreme summer heat (100-110F) that makes work difficult in the open high desert. Winter nights are cold, but daytime conditions are generally pleasant for outdoor collecting.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need to bring for opal digging in the Black Rock Desert?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar or crack hammer for working the friable host material, a brush or old toothbrush for cleaning specimens, and small buckets for collecting material. Essential supplies include sunscreen, a wide-brimmed hat, sunglasses, and at least 2-3 liters of water per person. Sturdy closed-toe shoes and light clothing are recommended for the high desert sun.

faq.question: Is Royal Peacock Opal Mines in Nevada good for beginner rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes - Royal Peacock is excellent for beginners and families. The fee dig format means you get access to prepared pit areas with exposed opal-bearing material, on-site staff can help you identify promising specimens, and the work is straightforward enough for children and inexperienced collectors to enjoy success. No prior rockhounding experience is necessary.

meta_title: Royal Peacock Opal Mines - Nevada Opal Digging
meta_description: Dig for precious and fire opal at Royal Peacock Opal Mines near Gerlach, Nevada. Fee dig site with excellent access for families and beginners.
